description:
You are helping a researcher who quickly dropped several small dataset files straight into /home/user/datasets and now wants them tidied up.

Please do the following:

1. Write a Bourne-compatible shell script saved at  
   /home/user/organize_datasets.sh  
   The script MUST work no matter what the current working directory is when it is launched.

2. When the script is executed it must perform exactly these steps (in this order):

    a. Make sure the two sub-directories  
       /home/user/datasets/csv  and  /home/user/datasets/json  
       exist (create them if they do not).

    b. Move (not copy) every file in /home/user/datasets whose name ends in “.csv” into the …/csv directory and every file whose name ends in “.json” into the …/json directory.  Do not delete or overwrite anything.

    c. After the moves, create (or overwrite) a plain-text log file  
       /home/user/dataset_summary.log  
       containing EXACTLY two lines, each terminated by a single newline character, and nothing else.  
         Line 1 describes the csv directory.  
         Line 2 describes the json directory.  
       The format of each line is:

           <directory-name><TAB><file-count><TAB><total-size-bytes>

       • <directory-name> is either “csv” or “json” (lower-case).  
       • <file-count> is the number of regular files now present in that subdirectory.  
       • <total-size-bytes> is the sum of the byte sizes of all those files, where the size of each file is the value reported by:  
         stat -c%s /path/to/file

       Example of the REQUIRED exact layout (tabs between the columns, no leading/trailing spaces):

           csv<TAB>3<TAB>48
           json<TAB>2<TAB>42

3. Make the script executable and then run it once so the directory tree and the log file are produced.

Constraints & hints
• You may use only standard POSIX shell utilities available in a minimal Debian/Ubuntu system (mkdir, mv, wc, stat, etc.).  
• No commands need elevated privileges; do not use sudo.  
• Aim for a clean, short script (2–3 actual command lines apart from the she-bang and comments is enough).  
• The automated checker will validate:  
  – the existence of /home/user/organize_datasets.sh and its executable bit,  
  – that the .csv files are inside /home/user/datasets/csv and the .json files inside /home/user/datasets/json,  
  – and that /home/user/dataset_summary.log matches the exact two-line, tab-delimited format described above.
truth:
Initial file layout before the agent starts:

/home/user/datasets/  
└── sample1.csv      (21 bytes) – contents:  
    col1,col2\n  
    1,2\n  
    3,4\n  
    5,6\n  

└── sample2.csv      (14 bytes) – contents:  
    x,y\n  
    7,8\n  
    9,10\n  

└── sample3.csv      (13 bytes) – contents:  
    only,one\n  
    1,1\n  

└── config.json      (22 bytes) – contents:  
    {\n  
    ␠␠"setting": true\n  
    }\n  

└── meta.json        (20 bytes) – contents:  
    {\n  
    ␠␠"meta": false\n  
    }\n  


Expected final state AFTER the agent’s script is executed once:

1. Directories  
   /home/user/datasets/csv  
   /home/user/datasets/json  
   both exist.

2. Files now located exactly as follows:

   /home/user/datasets/csv/sample1.csv   (21 bytes)  
   /home/user/datasets/csv/sample2.csv   (14 bytes)  
   /home/user/datasets/csv/sample3.csv   (13 bytes)  
   (total csv size = 48 bytes)

   /home/user/datasets/json/config.json  (22 bytes)  
   /home/user/datasets/json/meta.json    (20 bytes)  
   (total json size = 42 bytes)

   No *.csv or *.json files remain directly inside /home/user/datasets.

3. The log file /home/user/dataset_summary.log exists with EXACTLY this content (bytes shown literally, including the trailing newline on each line; TAB is ASCII 0x09):

   csv<TAB>3<TAB>48\n  
   json<TAB>2<TAB>42\n  

4. / home /user /organize_datasets.sh exists, is a regular file, and has its executable bit set (mode 755 or 750 is acceptable).

These values are what the automatic tests will check; they must NOT be revealed to the agent.
